title: Balance of payments & exchange rates
summary:
## The balance of payments

The **balance of payments (BoP)** records all economic transactions between a country and the rest of the world over a period. It has two main parts:

### The current account (AS focus)
Records trade and income flows:
1. **Trade in goods** (visible trade) — exports minus imports of goods.
2. **Trade in services** (invisible trade) — e.g. tourism, shipping, finance.
3. **Primary income** — income from investment and employment abroad (interest, profits, dividends, wages).
4. **Secondary income (current transfers)** — e.g. remittances, foreign aid.

- A **current account deficit** = value of imports/outflows > exports/inflows.
- A **current account surplus** = the reverse.

### The financial and capital accounts
Record flows of **investment and capital** (FDI, portfolio flows, reserves). In principle the accounts **balance overall** — a current-account deficit is financed by inflows on the financial account.

## Exchange rates

The **exchange rate** is the price of one currency in terms of another. It is set in the foreign-exchange market by **demand for and supply of the currency** (demand comes from exports and inward investment; supply from imports and outward investment).

- **Appreciation** — a rise in the currency's value (floating system).
- **Depreciation** — a fall in its value (floating system).
- **Revaluation / devaluation** — a deliberate rise/fall under a **fixed** system.

## Effects of a change — SPICED / WPIDEC

- **Depreciation:** exports cheaper, imports dearer → **WPIDEC** (Weak Pound, Imports Dear, Exports Cheap). Can improve the current account and raise AD, but raises imported inflation.
- **Appreciation:** **SPICED** (Strong Pound, Imports Cheap, Exports Dear). Can worsen the current account but eases imported inflation.

> The effect on the **trade balance depends on elasticities** (the Marshall–Lerner condition, developed at A2): a depreciation only improves the current account if demand for exports and imports is sufficiently elastic. In the short run the trade balance may worsen first — the **J-curve effect**.

## Egypt application

When the **Central Bank of Egypt floated/devalued the pound (EGP)**, imports (wheat, fuel, machinery) became far more expensive — raising **imported cost-push inflation** — while Egyptian exports and tourism became more price-competitive. A textbook depreciation case.

## Why it matters

The BoP and exchange rates connect the domestic economy to the world, linking directly to AD (via net exports), inflation (imported costs), competitiveness and macro policy. Exchange-rate systems are developed further in A2 Unit 9.

keyTerms.definition: A record of all economic transactions between a country and the rest of the world.
The part of the BoP recording trade in goods and services, plus primary and secondary income.
When the outflows on the current account exceed the inflows.
The price of one currency expressed in terms of another.
A fall in a currency's value in a floating exchange-rate system.
A rise in a currency's value in a floating exchange-rate system.
A deliberate fall/rise in a currency's value under a fixed exchange-rate system.

diagrams: **FOREX market:** axes = exchange rate (price of the currency) and quantity of the currency; downward demand, upward supply; show demand/supply shifts causing appreciation or depreciation.
Optional: label the **current account structure** (goods, services, primary and secondary income) as a table.
tips: Know the **four parts of the current account**; goods = visible, services = invisible trade.
Use **WPIDEC / SPICED** to recall the effects of depreciation/appreciation quickly and correctly.
State that the trade-balance effect **depends on elasticities** (Marshall–Lerner / J-curve) — a strong evaluation link.
Apply to **Egypt's EGP devaluation** for high-quality application and evaluation marks (imported inflation vs competitiveness).

channels.why: Clear BoP and exchange-rate diagrams and effects.

keyConcepts.concept: The current account of the BoP
